parts.text:
The nectar has changed its grammar.
The bees arrive at the foxglove
only to find the syntax rewritten—
a conversation started too early,
a punchline delivered to an empty room.

It is a fever of the calendar.
The soil is forgetting how to hold its breath;
it exhales its damp secrets into a sky
that has grown too hungry,
a sky that licks the moisture from the stone
like salt from a palm.

We are lengthening the shadows of the noon.
We have untethered the clock from the pendulum
and now the gears are spinning 
on the friction of our own skin. 
The porch swing no longer knows the rhythm 
of a cool evening; the wood stays warm 
long after the light has died,
holding a grudge of heat 
it was never meant to carry.

Listen to the birds:
their songs are migrating up the mountain,
climbing a ladder of degrees
until they run out of rungs. 
They are chasing a ghost of a season
that used to sit steady on the fence line.

It is not a crash, but a thinning.
The silk of the world is being pulled 
at both ends, the weave widening
until you can see the void through the threads.
We are stapling new months onto old maps,
demanding the earth speak a language
it never learned to breathe.

We are waking up in a house
where the walls have been moved 
two inches to the left every night.
It still looks like home,
until you try to walk through the door
and find the latch is gone.
